# FMEA — Camber Control Functional Chain
## Road Grader Front Axle Assembly — Logical Architecture
**Generated by:** SE Knowledge Partner (routine: fmea_from_functional_chain v1.0)
**Chain UUID:** 271a02ad-c196-4ebb-b966-109ab2c8d062
**Date:** 2026-06-17
**Source:** LA Camber Control functional chain fabric
**Prior FMEA cross-reference:** Steering Control (artifact 09e17c34)

---

## Scope

This FMEA covers all functional exchanges in the LA Camber Control functional chain. The camber system uses a single hydraulic actuator driving the RH knuckle directly, with the Camber Assembly transferring force symmetrically to the LH knuckle via mechanical linkage (design decision 2026-06-11). Severity, Occurrence, and Detection are scored 1–10. RPN = S × O × D.

**End effect reference:** Loss of camber angle correction at Receive LH/RH Camber (terminal functions — Left Wheel and Right Wheel actors).

**Shared component note:** Steering Knuckle LH, Steering Knuckle RH, Left Wheel Hub, Right Wheel Hub, and Kingpin Axle Assembly also appear in the Steering Control FMEA. Cumulative RPN for shared components is noted where relevant.

---

| Rank | # | Item | Failure Mode | RPN | Priority Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 16 | Steering Knuckle LH | Camber axis binding | **105** | Kingpin lubrication — covers both steering and camber chains |
| 2 | 17 | Steering Knuckle RH | Camber axis binding | **105** | Same — combined maintenance item |
| 3 | 7 | Camber Actuator | Retract seal failure | **100** | Seal inspection + leakage specification |
| 4 | 2 | Camber Valve Assembly | Partial extend signal | **96** | Minimum valve flow coefficient + camber angle sensor |
| 5 | 6 | Hydraulic System | Degraded supply pressure | **96** | Pressure monitoring + cross-chain flow competition check |

## Cross-Chain Analysis — Shared Components with Steering Control FMEA

| Component | Steering Control RPN | Camber Control RPN | Combined concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steering Knuckle LH | 80 (binding) | 105 (camber binding) | ★★ HIGH — same physical failure, two chains affected. Single kingpin lubrication failure disables both steering synchronization and camber correction on LH side |
| Steering Knuckle RH | 100 (cylinder force) | 105 (camber binding) | ★★ HIGH — RH knuckle is the most loaded node in the entire assembly. Receives cylinder force, tie rod, camber actuator, and camber assembly inputs simultaneously |
| Left Wheel Hub | 80 (deliver rotation) | 50 (camber output) | Moderate — hub must transmit both steering angle and camber correction. Combined interface failure affects both chains |
| Right Wheel Hub | 80 (deliver rotation) | 50 (camber output) | Moderate — same as LH hub |
| Kingpin Axle Assembly | 80 (stop engagement) | 60–72 (camber limits) | Moderate — hosts both steering stops and camber limit functions. Structural failure affects all four chains simultaneously |
| Hydraulic System | 108–140 (steering supply) | 60–96 (camber supply) | ★★ HIGH — shared hydraulic source. Simultaneous steering + camber demand not modeled as resource constraint. Peak demand scenario unanalyzed |

---

## Gaps Identified

**1. No camber angle mechanical stops in model**
Items 11, 13, 14, 15 all relate to unconstrained camber angle due to limit function failures. Unlike steering (which has Steering Stop Assembly with SC-011/SC-012 requirements), there is no physical camber stop modeled in the LA or PA layer. The `Limit LH/RH Camber` functions exist but have no physical enforcement mechanism. A camber stop or end-of-travel limiter should be added to the PA components.

**2. No camber angle property values on any component**
Camber control has no equivalent of SC-011 (maximum steering angle). No property value exists on Steering Knuckle LH/RH, Kingpin Axle Assembly, or Camber Actuator defining nominal, minimum, or maximum camber angles. S scores for camber over-travel items (11, 13, 14, 15) are based on engineering judgment — property values would allow quantitative scoring.

**3. Hydraulic system shared resource not modeled**
The Hydraulic System provides supply to both steering (Steering Hydraulic Valve) and camber (Camber Valve Assembly) simultaneously. Peak combined demand is not modeled as a constraint. A scenario where both systems demand maximum flow simultaneously (e.g. aggressive steering correction with simultaneous camber adjustment on rough terrain) has no FMEA coverage in either chain.

**4. Camber Actuator force property value missing**
Items 8 and 9 (actuator seizure and partial stroke) have subjective S/O/D scores. Adding minimum actuator force and rated pressure as property values on the Camber Actuator component would allow SC-006/007 equivalent requirements to be derived.

**5. Asymmetric camber failure modes require vehicle-level assessment**
Items 10 and 12 (transfer linkage fracture and Camber Assembly fracture) both produce asymmetric camber — one knuckle corrected, one not. The handling implications of asymmetric camber on a road grader (particularly on cross-slopes during grading operations) require vehicle-level analysis beyond the scope of this axle assembly FMEA.

---

## Recommended Next Steps

1. **Add camber mechanical stop** to PA layer (node component) and create equivalent requirements to SC-011/SC-012 for camber angle limits
2. **Add camber angle property values** to Steering Knuckle LH/RH and Camber Actuator
3. **Model hydraulic shared resource constraint** — add peak combined demand scenario to system-level FMEA
4. **Combine kingpin maintenance item** — items 16/17 in this FMEA and items 9/17 in Steering Control FMEA all require the same maintenance action. A single combined maintenance requirement covering both steering and camber kingpin lubrication should be authored
5. **Run FMEA comparison routine** (when available) — cross-chain RPN analysis for shared components Steering Knuckle LH/RH and Hydraulic System warranting a combined risk assessment
6. **Vehicle-level FMEA** — asymmetric camber failure modes (items 10, 12) escalated for vehicle-level analysis
